items.reason: This 223-year-old act embodies government-granted special privileges to a single private company, fostering cronyism and market distortions contrary to free enterprise principles. Modern general incorporation laws under the Companies Act suffice for all firms, rendering this obsolete. Retaining it imposes unseen costs: legal clutter increases compliance burdens, risks unequal treatment under law, administrative expenses for maintenance/interpretation, and potential barriers to competition by preserving archaic monopolistic perks. Deletion promotes institutional clarity, reduces regulatory deadweight, and aligns incentives with private property and liberty without harming operations, as the entity has long merged into modern firms.

items.title: House of Commons (Disqualifications) Act 1801
items.summary: The House of Commons (Disqualifications) Act 1801 extends pre-Union disqualifications from British and Irish parliaments to the new UK Parliament and prohibits certain Irish Crown office holders (e.g., customs/excise commissioners, contractors, revenue agents) from sitting as MPs after the then-current parliament, with penalties for violations, to curb executive influence via placemen.
items.reason: British citizens would be worse off without this legislation, as it prevents fusion of executive and legislative powers by barring direct Crown office holders and contractors from Parliament, preserving institutional separation that checks government overreach and cronyism; without it, incentives distort toward executive capture, eroding liberty and enabling rent-seeking that hampers prosperity, with no private mechanisms to replicate this safeguard.

items.title: House of Commons (Clergy Disqualification) Act 1801 (repealed)
items.summary: An Act from 1801 clarifying and enforcing the exclusion of persons in holy orders (clergy) from eligibility to sit in the House of Commons.
items.reason: This archaic law imposes an unnecessary restriction on political participation and liberty by disqualifying individuals based solely on their religious vocation, distorting representative democracy and individual rights without evidence of net benefits; unseen costs include reduced diversity in Parliament, potential chilling effect on civic engagement by religious individuals, and perpetuation of outdated church-state entanglements that hinder modern individualism and institutional efficiency.

items.title: Public Notaries Act 1801
items.summary: The Public Notaries Act 1801 regulates the admission and practice of public notaries in England, requiring individuals to be duly sworn, admitted, and enrolled in the appropriate court to act as notaries, primarily to prevent unqualified persons from practicing.
items.reason: This act enforces a government licensing regime that restricts entry into the notary profession, creating a supply bottleneck (only ~700 notaries for the UK), inflating fees (often £100+ per document), and limiting access for individuals and small businesses engaged in property transactions, international trade, and contracts. Unseen costs include distorted incentives for commerce, reduced entrepreneurship, and reliance on a cartel-like structure rather than market competition, reputation, liability insurance, and private certification, which would expand supply, lower costs, and maintain quality without state intervention.

items.title: Crown Debts Act 1801
items.summary: The remaining Section 9 of the Crown Debts Act 1801 establishes that officially printed statutes of England and Great Britain are conclusive evidence in Irish courts (pre-Republic), and Irish statutes printed by authorised printers are conclusive evidence in Great Britain courts, ensuring mutual recognition post-Union.
items.reason: British citizens would be worse off if deleted because it upholds legal certainty and rule of law by guaranteeing evidentiary admissibility of pre-Union official statute prints across UK jurisdictions; without it, disputes over historical laws could arise, distorting property rights and contracts reliant on clear statutory history, with no modern provision fully replicating this targeted mutual recognition and imposing zero compliance costs today.
